speaker: interviewer
text: This is a reel eight of the interview with Magda Blau. Magda, you were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we-- we been liberated on no-man's-land because we went on the march-- from the Russian they went. And we been walking toward the Czech meeting place. And so we arrived there, and from there, supposed the trucks take us to Prague. They took the Czech people, but they left us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Us being?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were 'us?'

speaker: interviewee
text: Us? The Jewish girls, because the Hungarian girls they went robbing again. They didn't like the Czechs. They put them Jew [? already, ?] so they didn't want to be in their company. So they wanted to be separated. And so we left with the Hungarians. But then, there was a-- a olderly lady, and she said, Magda, you have to do something. Otherwise, we will be left here forever in Germany. But we want to go home, see with there. So first I said, I swept enough, swept enough. Let somebody else see. This is a song, but I can't say it in the song way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, but listen, if you don't it, who will do it? Go to the committee. So I went to the committee, and I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are not taking away. And he said, we haven't got trucks. So I beat on the table, and I said, if I would be the cousin of President Benes, there will be straight-away truck. He looks at me. He got up, and he said, I am the cousin of President Benes. So I said, for you will be truck, but for us no? We been very good citizens of all the republic. They threw us out. They didn't look after us, and now, after all that-- what we went through, you don't have trucks for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, the first trucks who are coming you will be in it. So the-- a truck-- trucks arrived, and she said, you see, I told you that I have to send you. And so we came to Prague. And at Prague, we registered. They asked where you want to go. I said, after I found out what happens with my family, I want to go to Palestine. But in the same room, from the far end, somebody scream, Bela is alive. Because we didn't know about each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perhaps you could explain who Bela is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bela was-- was my future husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found out he was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And anyway, I was going to my hometown. I heard that our house stays. Then I took some girls who didn't have where to go. I took with them-- with me. And then I heard that Bela was, matter of fact, already there because he came earlier home. And he asked me to-- to come to [INAUDIBLE], where his sister is. I at first didn't want to go, you know, because I said, but I don't even know them. I don't know even him. But the-- one of the girlfriends who saved me from sicknesses, she said, you can't do that. He is waiting for you. You go, and then you still can't decide if-- whatever will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your-- did you know what happened to your parents at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At this stage, I found out, that they been taken the family with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with-- with my younger brother. The older brother was with them too, but he jumped from the walking-- running train. He jumped, and then he went to the partisans. My younger brother also went-- when he-- when they came to pick up our parent, he jumped from the-- how you call the top of the building. There is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Chimney?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The roof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the roof. Between the roof there is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had a room there over--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like an attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Attic, yeah. From the attic he jumped down on the garage. From the garage he disappeared, and I went to the partisan. A young boy-- he went to partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my young-- youngest brother. They went, and unfortunately, I heard when I came home, for one of them who did survive that my parents-- they-- they killed them out. They killed them out-- all. My father from beginning was working at the post office because he knew the languages, specially German. He was perfect in German. But then they decided they kill everybody, and they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you went to Bela's township?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there. He wasn't there. So I went again home. And then I came back, and then he came in and we met then. But we didn't stay there long. We went to Prague, and then we went-- because prisoners from Czech government got a certain entitlement to some either shop or whatever. So we went to a little village, and there we had a shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we married. There we married the 13th of March in '40-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you got married, and you had the little shop. And then what happened? How long did you stay in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stay long because they-- we builded up the shop very good. And they was jealous, so they wanted our shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: A person. And it started the Communist era in Czechoslovakia. So we saw that is no good to stay there. So it was a possibility to go to Israel. Even so, nobody wanted to go because the hardship was there. You been living in-- you will be living in-- in camps and in tents, and our children was two and a half and one and a half. But I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had children by that stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that stage, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- how many years did you stay, then, in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't matter how many years. It's all right. Let's go on from there. That doesn't-- it's not so important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Israel. What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Israel for seven years. And then we came to Melbourne--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what did you do in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we do? I opened the kindergarten, and I had a kindergarten, and it was lovely. And my husband was working at the city council in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you decide to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband wanted to come. He couldn't take because every month he had to go [INAUDIBLE] to the military, and this was too much for him. And also, he didn't like the heat. And also, I had the whole family here, and-- and they wanted, matter of fact, from beginning I should go with them to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, if everybody will go to Australia, we'll go to Israel. So I was always a Zionist. But why can't you come to Australia, make money, and then go with the money to Israel, I said. If everybody will say that, then who will start to do something in the Jewish state? So I went.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he said, let's send-- sned them the papers. In six week, we had the papers there, which normally they took two years. So he said, this is a sign we have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewer
text: In '56. And what did you do when you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard. We couldn't get work, so I was working part-time as a-- a saleslady. The story is too long, so I just say that, in American tailors as a saleslady.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, how will I manage? I can't speak-- speak the language. So he said, you can measure a neck of a man, a width of the man, and the length of the man. And so he said, you will do it, I know you will do it-- which I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, my husband was one stage working like a store man. And then we were-- was working on the Victoria market. And then we was working in Maya Licensed Grocery. And then, till the retirement, we was working in the boarding house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the market was it-- did you run your own store on the market?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, a big store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after that, you had your own businesses after that as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we had in-- and together with my daughter and son-in-law, we had the licensed grocery, which was named after our daughter, Maya Licensed Grocery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we are very proud grandparent of four grandchildren-- two girls and two boys the older one is already married, and she had her children, seven and five. So we are very proud great-grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you glad you made the decision to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you glad you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: From beginning, I wasn't because there I had all my friends. And also, I like the life in Israel. I was very happy there, and the children was so happy. And it was such a free life. And everybody-- the neighbors-- they screamed over. They came for coffee. They came for lunch. You know, this is a-- a friendly atmosphere, and specially in-- we didn't live in the city. We lived in a new settlement, so which was very nice. But then we had the children going to school, and they grew up. They went to Sunday school, and they went to the temple [INAUDIBLE]. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got accustomed to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you keep a traditional Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We are not ultra-religious, but we-- we belong to Elwood Synagogue, the whole family together in one. And we keep all the Shabbat and the holy days. And usually we have the family together for holy days and most of Saturday, Friday night, we are together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda, from your experiences, are there are any particular lessons that you want to tell people about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any particular lessons that you would like to tell people about from your experiences in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to tell to everybody to be physically and mentally thinking of the future and thinking and be prepared in something is happening-- that to watch the things that it should never happen again. And also, give the message because they are a people who wants to deny that that horrible happening, what I went through for three and half years, my husband for three years-- they want to deny that that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we have to do everything possible, and that's why I doing it. I am telling what happened. I didn't tell the terrible things because I don't want to upset people with the terrible thing. I just say certain things, but three and a half years, you can't put in such a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But my message is to be Jewish or believe in Israel, this is our Messiah, the state of Israel, the Knesset, the government. Because still we have Israel, we can breathe freely. You can speak up with something starts terrible. And we can't avoid it because we are able to speak up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think also that we should believe in Israel and help Israel should be strong. I did it all my youths, and I am glad I did it, even if I missed some other things. But now when I see, and I go to Israel, and I see what they did-- a lot of things, and that scientifically and economically and culturally, they built up in such a short time a beautiful state. What-- what does it make you think about people generally, your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it make you think about people generally, your experiences? Does it make you have thoughts about what people are like or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, it was very hard for us who survived because nobody asked how could you manage survive, and nobody's helpful and understanding when we came back from terrible thing. [DRINKS] We wouldn't lean on somebody. We couldn't talk to somebody because they thought that we must have-- do something terrible, or maybe we been friends with the Germans to be able to survive. But which is not true, which is not true. We had it very hard. And some girls was very young in charge, and naturally, sometimes they couldn't control. But they did what they could do best. So I-- I am trying to avoid that topic because if they are not interested, I'm not talking about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What makes you think you played the role you did play during the war, the role of someone that took charge of things, that helped in whatever way they could but wasn't passive? Why do you think you were like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you were like that? You weren't passive. You took charge of things or whatever situation you found yourself in, you took charge, you-- Why do you think you were like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I think it helped a lot that I was in the youths movement, active, and Zionist, and wanting the best for Jewish people. And wanting more people to survive because I believe we will have a Jewish state. And I was telling that to the people there. In the camp, keep together. When the war will be to end, we will have a state. So we need people to survive, so keep together. Help each other. This was one thing. But maybe that helped too that when I was a young girl, and my father took me to the [INAUDIBLE], a very famous rabbi, and when he blessed me and put his hand on my head, he said, this child has a special mission in her life. She's going to save hundreds and hundreds Jewish souls of-- of-- of man and woman. My father didn't understand. I was eight years old. He told my mother. They didn't understand. And they never told me about it because they didn't know what to think about it. They never heard about it. But when-- the same day, when we been already on the bus going to the train, so she stopped the bus because she wanted also to give me something. And she said, I must tell you, that and that happen when you been a little child. The rabbi bless you, and he said it. And I don't know if that wasn't like a-- I was hypnotized by that, that I have to-- a mission. And so I-- from the first minute, I kept the mission going. And I kept a motto-- only once you go to the chimney, but till you go, you have to do everything-- impossible, but you have to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda, are there any final words you'd like to-- to make?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish that somebody should look into it because I feel that nobody cared. That there was 20,000 young, beautiful girls, and from that survived about 400. But from the 400, a lot of them died on sicknesses what they had. And also, emotionally they was ruined because what they went through. But nobody made effort to look into it and give them, like-- like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] remembering them and talking about them. And take-- and-- and reliving with them a little bit what they lived through. And either they told the people they should know, because it's not enough if I tell you. It's not enough even I tell in the Holocaust Museum my making [? interview. ?] This is just a little thing. People should know, and maybe they will be aware that it could happen again if we are not cautious enough. Because how easy people could be incited. We have to be build ourself up to it, and it should never happen-- we should strengthen ourselves up and strengthen up the Jewish nation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Magda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda, who is in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Igna-- Igna Hellinger, and my [? mother, ?] my dear, darling mother-- bless her memory-- Berta [INAUDIBLE] Hellinger. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- sorry-- do you know what date it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can-- I can't tell you, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it taken very long before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war definitely, before the war. Maybe a year before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Michalovce.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Michalovce. Thank you. Would you tell us who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the photos are my three brothers and myself. The first is Maximilian [? Mordecai ?] Hellinger, who is 80-- 82. Then that's me [LAUGHS] and unrecognizable. And there is my younger brother, who is Eugen Hellinger, who died already two years ago. Then is Ernest Hellinger-- [INAUDIBLE] in Hebrew-- who died about five years ago. He died in Melbourne, and Eugen died in San Diego.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken here in Melbourne. It must be many years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all-- that's all right. Don't worry--

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photograph is my brother-- is Ernest, [INAUDIBLE], in Hebrew, [INAUDIBLE]. Unfortunately, he died in Melbourne five years ago,

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo was taken after the war in Michalovce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Straight after the war? Very quickly after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very quickly after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Few years after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few years after the war. OK, thank you. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- the Jewish synagogue in Michalovce. It was a very nice synagogue with beautiful windows. And we had a very nice hazzan and orchestra, like, singing, a boy's choir. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it still in the-- is the synagogue still in the town now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It's no synagogue there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's been destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it-- if it's destroyed, but it-- it's no synagogue there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: This--

speaker: interviewee
text: That--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the document which every returned soldier who was fighting for the victory of Czechoslovakia in the World War II got a medal and this paper to acknowledge his contribution to-- to the finishing of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got one? Did other Jews get one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other Jews in-- from concentration camps get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. But I got one, and it-- it has a-- a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A medal.

speaker: interviewee
text: A medal to what which I could wear it. And that's what-- I don't know if I should complain about it, but it-- it bugs me that the-- in Jewish-- if a non-Jew did save just one Jew, he is acknowledged. But if a Jew did-- in this case, I don't prize myself, but I knew I did incredible things. But I don't get anything, no acknowledgment

speaker: interviewer
text: No acknowledgment.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us who are in this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just the closest family. My husband, I am here. My younger daughter Eva, her husband David, my older daughter Maya, her husband Dez.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken the 23rd of March of '96, this year, to commemorate our 50th anniversary and also my husband 70-- 86th birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Brighton Savoy Motel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the photography is my first-born grandson Michael Irwine [? Itzhak ?] Lee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's 26.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Brighton Savoy on occasion of our 50th anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photograph is my very precious first-born granddaughter, Jennifer-- Jennifer Rachel, and she's now Fink. She's married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken to our 50th anniversary at the Brighton Savoy in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That--

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda, who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photograph is our second granddaughter, daughter of Eva and David Demsky, and her name is Amanda-- Amanda-- oh, I can't remember her other--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. Then when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amanda Demsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was also taken to the 50th anniversary of ours at the Brighton Savoy in Melbourne--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 23rd of March, '66.

speaker: interviewee
text: '96 [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this person?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the husband of my dearest granddaughter Jenny. His name is the Tony Fink, and he is the best person you can imagine. That-- picture was taken also at our 50th anniversary the 23rd of March, 1996 in Brighton Savoy Motel in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband of 50 years and hopefully many more together. His name is Bela Blau, and we lived many years happy together with our children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren after the having hard time what we went through during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in the camp under very special circumstances, which is, I think, somewhere mentioned, [INAUDIBLE] doesn't belong. He doesn't belongs in that segment. And it was about in August, 1944 when we saw each other for the first time. And after the war, we met again and we married about the [INAUDIBLE] Magda when the [INAUDIBLE] many, many [? things ?] 100,000 of people who came to the camp not [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] you can count on the-- on your one hand fingers that-- that-- who-- who [INAUDIBLE] he [INAUDIBLE] made so much in the-- in the interest of the [? myth ?] where [INAUDIBLE] other prisoners. My-- my first information which I asked about Magda when I met her the first time-- I asked a common friend to give me some kind of opinion. He told me, I can-- I can give you one-- in one single sentence-- in one single sentence, I can describe-- describe her. She is one of the very, very few people in the camp who saved a human being when she kept her full dignity, what very, very seldom happened in other cases.
